english: Japanese hot pot vegetables and tofu set

food_subgroup: Tofu and vegetable hot pot set

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for nabe (Japanese hot pot) with vegetables and tofu. FOODON_00002809 (hot pot food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for a composite nabe set. A0CKP (Tofu) is used as the closest parent anchor reflecting the primary protein component; vegetables are secondary components not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for nabe vegetables and tofu set as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 450 g, representing a full nabe set with tofu, mixed vegetables, and dashi broth as served at a Japanese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Tofu (silken/firm) and mixed vegetables composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Tofu, raw, firm (FDC ID 172476) and mixed vegetables (FDC ID 169967), weighted by typical nabe composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for nabe vegetables and tofu set exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for firm tofu (~40% by weight), mixed Asian vegetables including napa cabbage, shiitake mushrooms, carrot, and spring onion (~45% by weight), and dashi-based broth (~15% by weight). Macros reflect the combined edible portion per 100 g of the assembled dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of tofu and mixed vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on tofu GI (GI=32) from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a nabe vegetables and tofu set. GI is estimated at 32 based on the dominant protein-fat component (firm tofu, GI=32) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL. Mixed vegetables contribute negligible glycemic load. The composite dish GI is expected to remain in the low range (≤55) due to high protein, fat, and fibre content moderating glucose response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 20.25) / 100 ≈ 6.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from dashi broth and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that the dashi broth and soy sauce in nabe contribute approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 945 mg per serving), and should request reduced-sodium broth or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein and fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low glycemic index of approximately 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 6.5 per serving, this nabe set is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as part of a balanced meal, as the combination of tofu protein, dietary fibre from vegetables, and fat moderates postprandial blood glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based protein, and high vegetable content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish provides plant-based protein from tofu with low saturated fat (approximately 0.3 g per 100 g) and a favourable polyunsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio, supporting cardiovascular health; however, sodium from the broth should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nabe Vegetables and Tofu Set is a Japanese hot pot dish (nabe) consisting of firm or silken tofu simmered together with an assortment of vegetables — typically napa cabbage, shiitake and enoki mushrooms, carrot, and spring onion — in a light dashi-based broth seasoned with soy sauce and mirin. The dish is served as a set meal, often accompanied by steamed rice or udon noodles. The broth is delicate and umami-rich, the tofu is soft and absorbs the flavour of the dashi, and the vegetables retain a tender-crisp texture. Nabe is a traditional Japanese communal cooking style particularly popular in autumn and winter. Nutritionally, this set is a low-calorie, high-moisture dish providing moderate plant-based protein from tofu, dietary fibre from vegetables, and essential minerals including calcium, iron, and potassium. The glycemic index is low (approximately 32), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate due to the soy sauce and dashi broth, which is a consideration for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally cholesterol-free and low in saturated fat, making it a heart-friendly option. It is suitable for vegetarians when prepared with kombu dashi rather than fish-based dashi.
common_user_queries: Calories in Japanese hot pot tofu set
Japanese tofu vegetable hot pot nutrition

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for a nabe vegetables and tofu set exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or as a composite dish in USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for firm tofu and mixed Asian vegetables, which are well-characterised individually. The GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (tofu, GI=32) per Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-documented in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for firm tofu (FDC ID 172476, ~40% by weight), mixed Asian vegetables including napa cabbage, shiitake mushrooms, carrot, and spring onion (~45% by weight), and dashi-based broth (~15% by weight). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — tofu and vegetables contain no industrially produced trans fats; naturally zero by food composition). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the standard vegetarian preparation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for tofu and vegetables). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — tofu and vegetables contain no dietary cholesterol). vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA FoodData Central for this composite dish; mushrooms may contribute trace amounts of vitamin D but this is not quantified in the weighted composite estimation and the value is therefore set to -1 (missing/not reported) per the sentinel value convention. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record as tofu is the primary protein source and is classified under meat alternatives in Singapore dietary guidelines. The restaurant name 'GyoGyo - Funan' and menu code '405D' are preserved from the field survey source record for traceability. Serving size of 450 g is estimated based on typical Japanese restaurant nabe set portions. GL per serving = (32 × 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 4.5 portions equivalent) / 100 ≈ 6.5. is_vegan is false because standard dashi broth contains bonito (fish) flakes; a vegan version using kombu dashi is possible but not assumed for the restaurant context. is_halal is false as standard dashi may contain mirin (alcohol) and the restaurant halal status is unconfirmed.

Added Japanese local name '鍋野菜豆腐セット' to satisfy G6 multilingual requirement for Japanese cuisine.
Constructed composite nutrient estimate from USDA FoodData Central entries for firm tofu and mixed Asian vegetables with transparent weighting methodology.
Documented zero values for trans_fat_g, vitamin_b12_mcg, and cholesterol_mg with 'naturally zero' and 'confirmed zero' justifications in notes.

Revised notes to explicitly document vitamin_d_mcg = -1 with the phrase 'vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA FoodData Central for this composite dish', satisfying g3_numeric_hygiene sentinel value documentation requirement.
Selected GI=32 from Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed journal as analogous estimate for tofu-dominant dish.
